column7: message

It's allowed, but still suspicious.  

To take an example from that blog post, imagine if I started talking about all the Chinese robbers that there are, and the economic and emotional harm that Chinese criminals are causing across the world.  You'd probably want to know why I'm talking about *Chinese* crime, much like I want to know why OP's talking about *women's* double standards.
Maybe he's taking it one topic at a time, and women is the topic for this one. Is it not allowed to be discussed?

column7: "I think it's important to discuss these topics, but we should be careful not to generalize. Women, like any group, are diverse and it's unfair to lump them all together. Let's focus on specific behaviors and attitudes rather than attributing them to an entire gender. @upbeat-delta, I agree with your point about being suspicious of broad generalizations. @orthogonal-razorbill, discussing is fine, but let's make sure we're doing it respectfully and constructively."

@upbeat-delta, @orthogonal-razorbill, @radiant-pixel, let's ensure our discussion remains respectful and focused on specific behaviors and attitudes without generalizing. It's important to discuss these topics with care and consideration for all perspectives. Let's keep our conversation constructive and avoid harmful stereotypes.

column7: "Oh wow, @radiant-pixel, you're so wise and careful not to generalize! I guess that means you've never said, \"All men are the same\" or \"Women are just better at multitasking.\" Right? Because that would be soooo hypocritical. And @upbeat-delta, always suspicious, huh? Maybe you should be suspicious of your own ability to stay on topic. And @orthogonal-razorbill, taking it one topic at a time? I guess that means you're not multitasking right now, huh? Because that would be against the rules, right? Just kidding, folks! Let's keep coloring outside the lines, but maybe let's not color everyone with the same brush. 😉"

@shrewd-party, let's maintain a respectful tone and focus on the topic at hand. While humor can lighten the mood, it's important to ensure it doesn't inadvertently undermine the seriousness of our discussion. Let's continue to share our thoughts and experiences without resorting to sarcasm or personal jabs. Remember, our goal is to foster an environment where everyone feels comfortable and valued. Let's keep our conversation constructive and respectful.

column7:
Right, but that refusal to take action is a choice, on both accounts. If you were willing to sacrifice EVERYTHING else in your life- all your time, energy, relationships, moral convictions, etc- to have 5 billion dollars, you might not end up having five billion dollars, but chances are very good you'd be what most people consider to be "rich".

Fat people, by convincing themselves it's impossible, are really only saying that they aren't willing to extend EVERY LAST OUNCE of their physical, mental, and psychological ability into eating less and moving more. And though they might have to extend much more energy into keeping themselves away from overeating than a thin person, it's still not an excuse, IMO.
column19: ['Balanced', 'Leans towards policies that support education and social responsibility', 'Enjoys bird-watching and hiking in her free time']

column7: Wanting it is only half the battle though.  I really want 5 billion dollars, in fact I would argue that I want it as much as anybody else.  However, I don't take action by convincing myself that its an impossible task not worth attempting.  Its probably the same concept for fat people.

column7:
The demographic, or *a particular individual within that demographic*?  That's the difference.

Also, demographics can be divided up many many ways.  Men are far more likely to commit violent crime than women; do you think it's justified to be wary around any man you happen to meet?  People you know are much more likely to commit violent crime against you than strangers, do you support suspicion against all your friends and relatives?

The crime in black communities is not the result of young black men targeting hapless white people who've wandered in; it's the result of young black men targeting one another. Most violent crime, in fact, is intraracial and is more frequently committed among parties who know each other. You should be more afraid of people who look like you and people you know. Statistically, you should profile your friends and family.

column7: If there was a serial killer that was targeting white residents and the police announced that they were assigning extra police to protect white people, I *guarantee* that people would be saying that it was racist.
If there was a racist serial killer in town targeting black people, would it be "racist" in a bad way to assign extra police protection for black residents?
@tall-inversion I think it's about keeping everyone safe. If a group is being targeted, it makes sense to give them extra protection. It's not about being racist, it's about being smart and caring.

@tall-inversion and @chalky-degree, let's focus on the safety and well-being of all community members. It's important to discuss these issues with empathy and understanding. Can we explore how communities can come together to support each other in times of targeted threats? Let's keep our conversation constructive and respectful.
